item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was a novote at first, but I'll change it to an upvote.

Easy-to-make anomalies isn't my cup of tea, so I'm not that much in all this ease to produce memetic effects. I'm not a fan of SCP-181, either.

But, in the end, it was a very good story. Those things are just devices for a very simple, believable and amusing story, and it was well written. I can picture a researcher going insane at the Foundation, and this is pretty much how a believable insane person can looks like. He isn't dumb, there is logic to his conclusion, he isn't funny nor irrational. But the little unreal thought that formed led to his fate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering that they never let the guy conclude the supposed memes, we can't actually infer how long it would take for the killer image to form, even if he was deadly serious (pun intended). It could be as long as replicating a Da Vinci in ASCII.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad I stumbled across this one. Fun to read, and the Dr's logic is just feasible enough to make sense.

I especially like the little detail of his name changing to his SCP designation mid paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for cool overall idea and link to SCP-181

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: Version 5 of this article was reverted for making formatting changes without clear permission from the author or staff. Please don't perform any edit beyond corrections of errors in grammar, spelling or punctuation, and even then, consider that you may not be aware of the existence of a word, might not know why the author used a certain word etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Barley: So dramatic. This is completely believable, and definitely not you getting desperate.

From that whole interview, and especially from this line, I'm pretty sure there's part of Dr. Barley that's glad Krupin's in containment — even with how much trouble he's causing — because at least he's not in the same department anymore.

Anyway, I love Krupin. What a dick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Either Krupin's a moron with with sociopathic tendencies or he's gone totally insane after working for so long and thinks solitary confinement is the best thing ever like sensory deprivation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is brilliant- the best SCP I've read for a while. It kind of reminded me of the creepypasta Psychosis just because of the similar themes (paranoia, sealing yourself away from the world).

overall I thought it was superb. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What does he predict is going to happen that is worse than never seeing another human face or hearing another human voice again?

I'm pretty sure we could find a couple hundred fates worse than that on this wiki…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He knows 181 is lucky.
181 got solitary confinement.
For him, that means that solitary confinement is the best thing ever, since the lucky guy got it, it must be something good.

So, he makes himself an SCP that'll be forced into solitary confinement, thinking 'if the luckiest guy gets it, means getting it is good, I want it for myself too'

And does so by carving memetic symbols on himself and changing his voice so they have no other choice but putting him in solitary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't quite get this. So why did he carve the symbols into himself, and what does that have to do with 181?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ultimately, I don't think the 181 link is a problem. I mean, it's a gut reaction of "ermagerd crosstersts", but honestly, this is the kind of thing that works. That said, you could just as easily have blacked the number out. I'm not sure where it's at now, but what if someday 181 was removed and replaced with something else? This wouldn't make sense then. Granted, it's at least important to his dialogue that 181 be the skip mentioned, but it wouldn't mark the first time someone employed blackboxes while directly referencing another skip. Again, not a problem, I don't think.

As for solitary, I was under the impression that the whole reason this doctor did this to himself was to get into solitary confinement because he wanted what 181 had.

You interpreted the findings as indicating that the best situation for SCP-181 was in solitary confinement at the Foundation, so you decided to make yourself into an anomaly in order to get the same treatment.

And this implies that the doctor may be a little cracked for having done so.

…Buh, y'know what? I think I just talked myself into an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think this article requires that you feel anything positive about Krupin. For me it seems to hinge on asking if he is correct. For that him being unlikelable is a supporting element. It is harder to just assume someone you dislike, doing something abhorrent is actually making the most rational choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't personally find being able to personally identify with a character as a prerequisite to me following a narrative.

It's not, but if something's reception is based primarily on how someone feels about the characters involved… well, YMMV indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't personally find being able to personally identify with a character as a prerequisite to me following a narrative. I rather liked District 9 for example and the only really sympathetic character there was a reasonably incomprehensible alien. YMMV, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah "antihero" seems to be one of those weird words that is hard to define. Regardless I like non-sympathetic protagonists from time to time.

"Heroic" itself seems to have shifted in meaning over time. Achilles was a hero (in the original mythic sense) to the classical Greeks, I have always found him to be the most thoroughly unsympathetic character in the Iliad, even more than Agememnon.

Of course "villain" used to just mean "farmhand".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Part of what's at issue is that the most successful character-driven things have a sympathetic protagonist or supporting characters. Not saying the protagonist has to be a jerk with a heart of gold, but they need to be more than excessive "I'm mean!"

If the reader can't find any redeeming qualities of an asshole-ish character, they cannot connect with the character outside of wanting to metaphorically tear their spine out and beat them to death with it. If this is the protagonist, then the work has to make it up elsewhere, maybe by making the other characters a lot worse, or by introducing a sympathetic antagonist.

Here, it looks like the attempt was made for the reader to sympathize with Dr. Barley, but he comes off as too unfeeling. So, our choice ends up connecting with someone who has no apparant redeming qualities, or someone with the emotional depth of tinfoil. Giving Barley too much depth would come accross as him being too unprofessional, at least for interviews, so the only choice is to try and inject sonething humanizing into Krupin.

And I'm just not seeing that anywhere in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would sooner call him a villain protagonist than an anti-hero. Anti-heroes tend to be, well, heroic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't there a Catch-22 there? Fiction in which everybody, even the most antisocial narrcisists are likable is unrealistic and you should downvote for that.

Making this guy a likable paranoid sociopath would probably have me, at least, less receptive to it. I also don't like the Clarice Starling books after Silence of the Lambs for the same reasons.

I guess de gustibus non est disputatum but I find actual antihero protagonists to be a refreshing change from relentlessly likable heroes and sympathic glib villians.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hence my downvote. The article, as it stands, hinges on the fact that the guy is portrayed as unlikable.

And, as crazy as it sounds, I do not like unlikable characters. And if the article hinges on said unlikability…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't he supposed to be unlikable?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Those promised additional thoughts:

+ show block
– hide block

I ended up liking this. I like the characterization, but I also like how the conversation was somewhat balanced between the two doctors. I mean, it was still a giant exposition dump, but it included enough back-and-forth with Dr. Barely to keep it from being excessively one-sided. You also make it fairly clear (imho) that 1: Konstantin isn't as clever as he thinks he is and 2: Barley thinks he's a prick.

As to the most unbelievable parts, this is very hard-meme, if we were to posit a scale ranging from soft- to hard-meme in the style of low- versus high-magic fantasy. That's not necessarily a bad thing, but it is the only card that gets played. And it gets played a lot. Oddly enough, the thing I found least believable wasn't the vocal-cord modification (which I mostly thought was gross), it was the idea of a kill-meme transmittable through a basic chat interface. I mean, ok, in my headcanon of memes you could probably ascii one up with like thirty perfectly set lines lines, but that's about it. (SCP-2999 did anomalous things with plain text, but explicitly had non-standard characters.)

It's left ambiguous whether or not the text is deadly. I prefer to believe that he's full of shit, but if I have a complaint it's that it's somewhat far towards it being real for me. Can't suggest an improvement, just my reaction.


Anyhow, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He could also be a sociopath. They can stop being likeable pretty damn quick when they know they no longer need you or know they can get away with it. Also fits with the "I don't care how many people die getting me into solitary" reasoning.

If this behavior isn't an isolated incident, he probably qualifies pretty strongly for antisocial personality disorder. Impulsivity, irresponsibility, and reckless disregard for human life are all diagnosable traits there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, like I said, it's a solid article otherwise, and if he showed at least an understand of "ok, this probably wasn't the way I should have done this", I'd probably be upvoting without hesitation.

It's just a risk with character-driven SCPs like this. If someone finds the character unlikable, then they downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. How many people would throw their friends under the bus if they thought it was their only chance at salvation, or just because they got upset?

As I explained upthread, I don't imagine him as being in a good state of mind, and the fact that people would probably not perfectly play along with his plan probably wasn't at the forefront of his mind, and he might have been genuinely surprised to hear that they were going to try and render him safer.

If I said any more, I would have to pull it out of my ass; if this isn't a satisfying justification of how I wrote this article, then I'll take the downvote without objection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A: Yes, that was the SCP I was thinking of.

B: I think my reaction is less "they're an asshole" and more "they threw a tantrum". It's one thing for Krupin to turn himself into a walking kill-agent, that's not bugging me. What's bugging me us that he's basically thrown a tantrum, knowing full well that the Foundation would do everything theycan unlock his notes at some point and still refusing to allow ex-collegues to safely access them, and to top it all off, threatening to go Keter on the entire Foundation if they try. These were presumably his friends, and he's purposefully throwing them under a bus knowing full well that they have almost no say in the matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was SCP-2357, I suspect.

As for Krupin's assholery, he's just transfered from researcher to SCP in a very short period of time. It's very clear to him that social expectations of him are very different now — why not flex that a bit and mouth off to a former coworker, especially one who used to treat him like a colleague, but now won't even treat him as human?

Not that I would morally justify any of this, and if it's just not something you enjoy reading then I can't argue with that, but I think professionalism is far more an aspect of the job than of anyone in it, and you can't expect it to carry on past the job's end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suspect the Memetics Division is filled with assholes. It's probably why the Orientation now has a caveat basically saying, "If you can hear this, we know you're an asshole. Stop it."

Heck, if I like faking out people and making them think what I want them to think, then Memetics Researcher looks like a really good career path.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fakeout collapsible was something I pioneered to get around the issue of nested collapsibles in SCP-2122; I realized it could operate as a less annoying version of a real collapsible, and I plan on continuing to use it for future endeavors.

I'm not sure I understand the rest of your comment, though. Where is it implied that solitary is "cushy"? I also think it's very relevant to containment to list that it's intimately familiar with the Foundation, in case it ever escapes.

The 181 connection is… it's fine if that's not to your liking, but I can't really work with an unelaborated "not sure about" one way or another — whether your unsureness has good reasons, bad reasons, or purely personal reasons. So I'm inheriting your unsureness here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted for… basically, the guy being an asshole, like that other memeticist. Forgot the number- but it was basically a crappy award thing that compelled people to frame and hang on a wall.

It's one thing if the SCP is naturally an asshole… but this used to be a Foundation Researcher, just like that other one. I guess I expect a bit more professionalism?

Solid article otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. Turning yourself into a skip so you can get "cushy" solitary confinement seems like a dumb idea. I'm not sure about the 181 connection. I almost downvoted for "it knows all our secrets", but that at least is explained well. (I really like that fakeout collapsible, by the way.) But overall, I'm not sure about this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Death, perhaps? A fate worse than that?

Some people don't think that they need other people. It's a disturbingly common cultural trend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Getting locked in solitary confinement forever just for a theory? What does he predict is going to happen that is worse than never seeing another human face or hearing another human voice again?

I can't picture anyone at all doing this, hence the downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I very much enjoy the fact that you wrote a story about a Foundation researcher carving kill-symbols into their skin without the researcher actually "going crazy" in the stereotypical horror sense.

Heh, I suppose that is kind of a subversion.

That's not to say that Krupin is totally unaffected by his work with memes. I tend to write experienced memeticists as highly emotional and with poor judgment because, in my headcanon, workplace safety is a lot harder when you don't always have the clearest idea of what you're working with — poring over borderline-anomalous memes, over time, sort of fries the processes of the brain involved with logically processing things, so they make logical leaps and come up with weird plans based on their intuition.

But you're right in that it's definitely a different brand of not being in the best state of mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's definitely the least believable part of the SCP; perhaps the fact that he could do so is anomalous in its own right, perhaps he just happened to have an incident report from "the wild" which described how someone did it. If he'd given himself some sort of (memetic) sedative, keeping calm and steady wouldn't be an issue at least.

As for not just having him say the right words to get the effect… maybe he can't. At that point it's basically straight mind control, and it would beg the question of why he didn't just tell the Foundation to lock him up. I wanted a higher barrier of entry, so to speak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I've seen a cross-link like this before, but it's very effective and adds a lot of interest to the cross-linked SCP without trying to reimagine it.

It has certain flaws (the vocal cords part is definitely stretching credibility), but overall it's really quite solid - even the (conceptual) weakness of Krupin's anomaly makes sense, given that it's crude, blatant DIY memetics designed only to get him locked up. Krupin's a weird, unlikable asshole, but his characterisation as such is strong. I very much enjoy the fact that you wrote a story about a Foundation researcher carving kill-symbols into their skin without the researcher actually "going crazy" in the stereotypical horror sense.

+1. I'd definitely be interested in seeing more stories like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It also seems unnecessary. Voice is already a memetic carrier. He just needs the right words.

I still like this one a lot though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He accurately modified his own vocal cords with an ordinary knife to obtain a memetic voice. That sounds like a rather hard thing to do. I see that you wanted this guy to equip himself with memetic agents, and while carving symbols to his face is kind of believable, this is just ridiculous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As for the right idea:

+ show block
– hide block

I wondered about that. The fact that solitary confinement is potentially best for 181 doesn't mean that solitary confinement is best. For example, something that kills everyone in the site might just happen to pass over 181's solitary cell, not all of them.

But as you suggest, Krupin is an extremely arrogant self-centered prick, so he both believes the threat to him is paramount and is certain that he has the perfect solution. It's clever on your part that he thinks the threat is memetic, because of course he does.

I haven't voted yet and may have other thoughts on this, but I'll get to them later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because 181 apparently has perfect luck and that's what happened to 181. Krupin interpreted that as "Solitary confinement at the Foundation is the best place to be" and he went for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but I'm confused as to the doc's motivation and how 181 factored into things.

can someone explain why he carved himself up for the sole purpose of getting into solitary?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the fact that the crosslinking is sort of an in-universe response to something, while still allowing the article to stand on its own. Sort of runs with the concept and ramifications of the other article in a way that makes the setting feel deeper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good-ish concept that tells an excellent story. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Props to TwistedGears and Soulless for suggesting changes, others in chat for rubber-stamping it, cyrus013 and pooryoric for making SCP-181 what it is today, and a post Sorts made back in 2011 for giving the inspiration for this article.

This article is sort of intended to function as a continuation of 181's story, but I tried to make it so that you don't have to have any knowledge of 181 for the article to work.

Does Krupin have the right idea?
Sort of.

There is definitely the possibility that 181 is in solitary confinement for its own good, and solitary confinement at the Foundation is the safest place to be in 181's lifetime. There's also any number of other possibilities — namely, that 181's solitary confinement is only the best option for 181, or that 181's probability-affecting nature doesn't extend to that, or… you get the picture. Krupin, however, is very paranoid and very self-centered, so when he looked through 181's documentation, he interpreted it as writing on the wall and decided to do whatever it takes to get the same treatment.

Only time can tell if he was wise in doing so.


Fun fact
Krupin is derived from the West Slavic 'krupa' meaning 'barley'.
